content:
# Luke Chapter 4 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Chapter:** Luke 4 (44 verses) | **Date:** 2026-03-04

---

## Summary
- **Total verses:** 44
- **Verses with disagreements:** 12 (vv. 1, 2, 3, 5, 13, 21, 22, 23, 27, 29, 32, 38)
- **Nature of disagreements:** All stylistic/translational; no manuscript-reading disputes
- **Textual-critical unanimity:** All three agents agreed on every Sinaiticus-specific reading (shorter text at v. 4, temptation order, omission of "Get behind me Satan" at v. 8, "unrolled" at v. 17, "Judea" at v. 44)

---

## Detailed Disagreements

### Verse 1 — "full of" vs "overflowing with"
| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "full of [the] Holy Spirit" |
| LOGOS | "full of the Holy Spirit" |
| PNEUMA | "overflowing with the Holy Spirit" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"full of the Holy Spirit"** |

**Ruling:** PNEUMA's "overflowing" introduces interpretive amplification beyond *pleres* (full). While evocative, it overstates the Greek. EMET and LOGOS agree on the standard rendering, which SOPHIA adopts.

---

### Verse 2 — "hungered" vs "was hungry" vs "was starving"
| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "he hungered" |
| LOGOS | "he hungered" |
| PNEUMA | "he was starving" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"he was hungry"** |

**Ruling:** *Epeinasen* (aorist of *peinao*) means "he became hungry / he hungered." PNEUMA's "starving" exceeds the semantic range. EMET/LOGOS's "hungered" is slightly archaic for modern readers. SOPHIA selects "was hungry" as natural and accurate.

---

### Verse 3 — "speak to" vs "command" vs "tell"
| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "speak to this stone that it might become bread" |
| LOGOS | "command this stone to become bread" |
| PNEUMA | "tell this stone to become bread" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"command this stone to become bread"** |

**Ruling:** Greek *eipe* (second aorist imperative of *lego*) literally means "say/speak," but in context it functions as a command directed at the stone. LOGOS's "command" conveys the supernatural directive force. PNEUMA's "tell" is too casual. EMET's "speak to" is literal but misses the commanding register.

---

### Verse 5 — "inhabited-world" vs "world"
| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "all the kingdoms of the inhabited-world" |
| LOGOS | "all the kingdoms of the world" |
| PNEUMA | "all the kingdoms of the world" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"all the kingdoms of the world"** |

**Ruling:** *Oikoumene* technically means "the inhabited world" (the Roman-era known world). EMET's compound "inhabited-world" is precise but clumsy in English. "World" communicates the scope effectively and is the standard rendering. LOGOS and PNEUMA agree.

---

### Verse 13 — "a season" vs "an opportune time" vs "a more opportune time"
| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "until a season" |
| LOGOS | "until an opportune time" |
| PNEUMA | "until a more opportune time" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"until an opportune time"** |

**Ruling:** *Achri kairou* is "until a season/time." EMET's "season" is technically correct but vague in modern English. PNEUMA's "more opportune" introduces a comparative not present in the Greek. LOGOS's "an opportune time" accurately conveys *kairos* (a fitting, appointed season) without addition.

---

### Verse 21 — "in your ears" vs "in your hearing"
| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "fulfilled in your ears" |
| LOGOS | "fulfilled in your hearing" |
| PNEUMA | "right here as you listen, this scripture has come true" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"fulfilled in your hearing"** |

**Ruling:** Greek *en tois osin humon* is literally "in your ears." EMET preserves the body-part idiom, which is slightly unusual in English. PNEUMA paraphrases expansively, losing the economy of the original. LOGOS's "in your hearing" renders the idiom naturally while preserving its structure.

---

### Verse 22 — "bearing witness" vs "spoke well of"
| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "all were bearing-witness to him" |
| LOGOS | "all bore witness to him" |
| PNEUMA | "everyone spoke well of him" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"all spoke well of him"** |

**Ruling:** *Emarturoun auto* (imperfect of *martureo* + dative) in this context means "testified favorably about him" — i.e., they spoke approvingly. The "witness" language in English suggests legal or solemn testimony, which misses the Lukan nuance of popular approval. PNEUMA captures the contextual sense best. SOPHIA uses "all" (with EMET/LOGOS) rather than PNEUMA's "everyone" for consistency.

---

### Verse 23 — "parable" vs "proverb"
| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "this parable" |
| LOGOS | "this parable" |
| PNEUMA | "this proverb" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"this proverb"** |

**Ruling:** *Parabole* has a broad semantic range: parable, comparison, proverb, figure of speech. "Physician, heal yourself" is a proverbial saying, not a narrative parable. PNEUMA correctly identifies the contextual meaning. Rendering as "parable" in English would mislead readers into expecting a story.

---

### Verse 27 — "Naiman" vs "Naaman"
| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "Naiman the Syrian" |
| LOGOS | "Naaman the Syrian" |
| PNEUMA | "Naaman the Syrian" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Naaman the Syrian"** |

**Ruling:** Sinaiticus spells the name NAIMAN, a Greek transliteration variant of the Hebrew. EMET faithfully reproduces the manuscript spelling. However, English Bible convention universally uses "Naaman" (from 2 Kings 5), and retaining "Naiman" would cause unnecessary confusion without adding textual insight for general readers. The variant is noted.

---

### Verse 29 — "city" vs "town"
| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "their city" |
| LOGOS | "their city" |
| PNEUMA | "their town" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"their city"** |

**Ruling:** Greek *polis* is consistently rendered "city" in formal translation. While Nazareth was a small settlement, the text uses *polis*, and SOPHIA maintains lexical consistency with the Greek term. EMET and LOGOS are followed.

---

### Verse 32 — "was with authority" vs "carried authority"
| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "his word was with authority" |
| LOGOS | "his word was with authority" |
| PNEUMA | "his words carried authority" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"his word carried authority"** |

**Ruling:** Greek *en exousia en ho logos autou* — literally "in authority was his word." SOPHIA combines PNEUMA's more dynamic verb "carried" (which better conveys that authority inhered in the speech itself) with EMET/LOGOS's singular "word" (*logos*, singular in the Greek). The result is both faithful and expressive.

---

### Verse 38 — manner of the request for healing
| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "they asked him concerning her" |
| LOGOS | "they made request of him concerning her" |
| PNEUMA | "they asked Jesus to help her" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"they appealed to him on her behalf"** |

**Ruling:** Greek *erotao* means to ask or entreat, and *peri autes* means "concerning her." EMET is flat; LOGOS is formal but stilted; PNEUMA adds "to help" which is not in the text. SOPHIA's "appealed to him on her behalf" captures the entreating quality of *erotao* and the indirect *peri* construction without adding content.

---

## Areas of Full Agreement (no disagreement)

The following significant textual and translation decisions were **unanimous** across all three agents:

1. **v. 4** — Shorter reading (omit "but by every word of God") per Sinaiticus
2. **vv. 5-8, 9-12** — Lukan temptation order preserved (kingdoms second, temple third)
3. **v. 8** — Omission of "Get behind me, Satan"
4. **v. 17** — "unrolled" (*anaptuxas*) for scroll, not "opened"
5. **vv. 18-19** — Composite Isaiah quotation (61:1-2 + 58:6) preserved
6. **v. 33** — Full phrase "spirit of an unclean demon" retained
7. **v. 34** — "Ha!" for the interjection *ea*
8. **v. 41** — "they knew him to be the Christ" per Sinaiticus
9. **v. 44** — "Judea" (*Ioudaias*) retained over Byzantine variant "Galilee"
